map.condition: Binary search on sorted array, worst-case comparisons
Insertion sort number of swaps / comparisons from inversions
Hash table open addressing, linear probing load factor

map.use: \Omega(n \log n) \text{ comparisons required by any comparison-based sort}

formulas.name: Binary Search Recurrence
formulas.whenToUse: Counting maximum comparisons in recursive binary search on a sorted array of size n

terms.means: max comparisons on array of size n
number of elements in the sorted array

formulas.trap: GATE sets F(1)=1 (one comparison for a single element) vs F(1)=0 depending on the model; read the question carefully — the recurrence should have F(n)=F(floor(n/2))+1 and F(1)=1 gives Theta(log n)

formulas.name: Binary Search Time Complexity
formulas.latex: T(n) = \Theta(\log_2 n) \text{ (worst case)}, \quad T(n) = O(1) \text{ (best case)}
formulas.plain: Worst case: Theta(log2 n), Best case: O(1)
formulas.whenToUse: Stating time complexity of binary search on a sorted array
formulas.trap: Binary search requires a sorted array — it does NOT work on a linked list (no O(1) random access) or unsorted arrays. Questions often test whether you confuse average and worst case.

formulas.name: Arithmetic Operations in Recursive Binary Search
formulas.plain: Arithmetic ops = Theta(log2 n)
formulas.whenToUse: When the question asks for arithmetic operations (mid computation, comparisons) rather than memory use
formulas.trap: Counting arithmetic operations is the same order as comparisons; both are Theta(log n) in the worst case for binary search.

formulas.name: Insertion Sort Standard Complexity
formulas.latex: T(n) = O(n^2) \text{ (worst)}, \quad T(n) = O(n) \text{ (best — already sorted)}
formulas.plain: Worst: O(n^2), Best: O(n) for already-sorted input
formulas.whenToUse: Standard insertion sort with linear search for insertion position
formulas.trap: Best case is O(n), not O(1). Worst case is reversed-order input, not just 'random' input.

formulas.name: Insertion Sort and Inversions
formulas.plain: Number of swaps = number of inversions; max = n*(n-1)/2
formulas.whenToUse: When the question gives inversions or asks how many swaps/comparisons insertion sort makes on a specific permutation

terms.means: number of pairs, maximum possible inversions

formulas.trap: Comparisons and swaps are related but not equal — the number of comparisons >= number of swaps. Swaps equal inversions exactly in the standard adjacent-swap version.

formulas.name: Insertion Sort with Binary Search for Position
formulas.plain: Comparisons: O(n log n) using binary search; shifts/moves still O(n^2)
formulas.whenToUse: When the question replaces linear search with binary search to find insertion point in insertion sort

terms.means: reduced to O(n log n) by binary search
element moves to create gap — still O(n^2)

formulas.trap: Using binary search only reduces comparisons, NOT the total running time — the array-shift cost is still O(n^2), so overall complexity remains O(n^2).

formulas.name: Comparison-Based Sorting Lower Bound
formulas.latex: \Omega(n \log n) \text{ comparisons required by any comparison-based sorting algorithm}
formulas.plain: Any comparison sort needs Omega(n log n) comparisons
formulas.whenToUse: When asked why merge/heap sort is optimal, or whether a comparison sort can beat O(n log n)
formulas.trap: This lower bound applies ONLY to comparison-based sorts. Counting sort, radix sort, bucket sort bypass it by using key structure rather than comparisons.

formulas.name: Merge Sort Complexity
formulas.latex: T(n) = 2T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + O(n) \Rightarrow T(n) = O(n \log n) \text{ (all cases)}
formulas.plain: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) => O(n log n) in all cases
formulas.whenToUse: Merge sort on arrays — worst, average, and best all Theta(n log n)
formulas.trap: Merge sort is NOT in-place (needs O(n) auxiliary space). Its best case is still Theta(n log n), unlike insertion sort.

formulas.name: Quick Sort Complexity
formulas.plain: Average: O(n log n); Worst: O(n^2) (sorted or reverse-sorted input with fixed pivot)
formulas.whenToUse: Quick sort analysis, pivot selection, best vs worst case

terms.means: occurs when pivot is always min or max element

formulas.trap: The worst case of quick sort is O(n^2) when the input is already sorted and pivot is chosen as first/last element. Randomized pivot makes worst case unlikely but not impossible.

formulas.name: Heap Sort Complexity
formulas.plain: O(n log n) all cases, in-place
formulas.whenToUse: When both O(n log n) worst-case and in-place are required simultaneously
formulas.trap: Heap sort is not stable. Merge sort is stable but needs O(n) space. Heap sort is in-place but not stable.

formulas.name: Counting / Radix Sort Complexity
formulas.latex: \text{Counting sort}: O(n+k),\quad \text{Radix sort (d digits, base k)}: O(d(n+k))
formulas.plain: Counting sort: O(n+k); Radix sort: O(d*(n+k))
formulas.whenToUse: Integer keys in range [0,k]; bypasses comparison lower bound
formulas.trap: Counting/radix sort is linear only when k=O(n). If k is very large (e.g. k=n^2), counting sort is NOT better than O(n log n) sorts.

formulas.name: Stability and In-Place Properties
formulas.plain: Insertion: stable, in-place. Merge: stable, NOT in-place. Quick: NOT stable, in-place. Heap: NOT stable, in-place. Selection: NOT stable, in-place.
formulas.whenToUse: Questions about whether an algorithm is stable or in-place

terms.means: equal elements maintain original relative order

formulas.trap: Merge sort can be made in-place but then loses O(n log n) or stability guarantees. Standard merge sort is NOT in-place.

formulas.name: Division-Method Hash Function
formulas.plain: h(k) = k mod m
formulas.whenToUse: Standard hash function mapping key k to slot in table of size m

terms.means: number of slots/buckets in hash table

formulas.trap: Choose m to be a prime not close to a power of 2 for best distribution. m = power of 2 only uses the low-order bits of k.

formulas.name: Load Factor
formulas.plain: alpha = n / m
formulas.whenToUse: Measure of how full a hash table is; drives expected performance

terms.means: number of slots in hash table
load factor; for chaining alpha can exceed 1; for open addressing alpha < 1

formulas.trap: For open addressing, alpha must be strictly less than 1. For chaining, alpha can be > 1 without failure.

formulas.name: Hashing with Chaining — Expected Complexity
formulas.plain: Unsuccessful search: O(1 + alpha); Successful search: O(1 + alpha/2)
formulas.whenToUse: Expected time analysis of hash table with separate chaining under simple uniform hashing assumption
formulas.trap: Worst case of chaining is O(n) when ALL keys hash to same bucket. GATE often asks for worst case vs average case.

formulas.name: Linear Probing Probe Sequence
formulas.plain: h(k,i) = (h'(k) + i) mod m
formulas.whenToUse: Resolving collisions by probing next sequential slot
formulas.trap: Linear probing suffers from primary clustering — long runs of occupied slots form, degrading performance well before alpha reaches 1.

formulas.name: Linear Probing Expected Probes
formulas.plain: Unsuccessful: (1 + 1/(1-alpha)^2)/2; Successful: (1 + 1/(1-alpha))/2
formulas.whenToUse: Expected number of probes for linear probing as a function of load factor
formulas.trap: As alpha approaches 1, expected probes blow up — table must not be completely full. At alpha=0.5 expected unsuccessful probes ~ 2.5.

formulas.name: Quadratic Probing Probe Sequence
formulas.latex: h(k, i) = \left(h'(k) + c_1 i + c_2 i^2\right) \bmod m
formulas.plain: h(k,i) = (h'(k) + c1*i + c2*i^2) mod m
formulas.whenToUse: Resolving collisions with quadratic step to reduce primary clustering
formulas.trap: Quadratic probing avoids primary clustering but suffers from secondary clustering — keys with same h'(k) follow same probe sequence.

formulas.name: Double Hashing Probe Sequence
formulas.plain: h(k,i) = (h1(k) + i*h2(k)) mod m
formulas.whenToUse: Best open-addressing collision resolution — avoids both primary and secondary clustering

terms.means: primary hash function, e.g. k mod m
secondary hash function, e.g. 1 + (k mod m')

formulas.trap: h2(k) must never evaluate to 0 (would cause infinite loop on same slot). Common choice: h2(k)=1+(k mod (m-1)) or h2(k)=1+(k mod (m')) for m' slightly less than m.

formulas.name: Double Hashing Typical GATE Setup
formulas.latex: h_1(k) = k \bmod 11,\quad h_2(k) = 1 + (k \bmod 7),\quad h(k,i) = (h_1(k) + i \cdot h_2(k)) \bmod 11
formulas.plain: h1(k) = k mod 11, h2(k) = 1 + (k mod 7), h(k,i) = (h1(k) + i*h2(k)) mod 11
formulas.whenToUse: Standard GATE double hashing example with m=11, m'=7
formulas.trap: Compute h2(k) first to get the step size, then add multiples for each collision. The '+1' in h2 ensures step size is never 0.

formulas.name: Linear Search Complexity
formulas.plain: Worst: O(n), Average: O(n/2) = O(n)
formulas.whenToUse: Sequential search through unsorted or sorted array element by element
formulas.trap: Linear search works on unsorted arrays; binary search requires a sorted array. A sorted array does NOT require binary search — linear search also works, just slower.

formulas.name: Binary Search Prerequisites
formulas.latex: \text{Requires: sorted array + O(1) random access (not linked list)}
formulas.plain: Requires sorted array with O(1) index access (arrays only, not linked lists)
formulas.whenToUse: Determining applicability of binary search to a given data structure

terms.means: possible only on arrays, not linked lists

formulas.trap: Binary search on a linked list has O(n) worst case even if sorted, because finding the midpoint requires O(n) traversal. So it is no better than linear search on a linked list.
